summary: I came onto the waterfront in Portland, Oregon in 1929 during the Depression. We lived in a small four-room house, and work was scarce, so I took any job I could get, even if it paid very little. Portland was where I first got involved with waterfront work and learned about union struggles from the older men in the gangs. Later, in 1931, we started reorganizing the union membership in Portland, though it was difficult to hold onto it until the Wagner Act was passed.

I started working on the waterfront in 1929, right in the middle of the Great Depression. Jobs were almost impossible to find as businesses were closing down everywhere. I was 24, married with two kids, and desperate to find any work to feed my family. The Depression made life very hard, but it also motivated me to get involved in union organizing for better protection in the future.

Ory Cole was an old friend I ran into on the Portland waterfront when I first started in 1929. He was working in a steady gang and helped me get a job filling in with the Pete Hendrickson gang. Ory was part of the group of older men who shared their experiences about past strikes and union struggles, which shaped my understanding and determination to help build a union.

Pete Hendrickson was the leader of a gang on the Portland waterfront where I first got work filling in. His gang was one of the preference gangs where extra work would come along and younger men like me could get a chance to work. Being part of his gang introduced me to the realities of waterfront work and the challenges older workers faced as they were often let go for slowing down.

There was no Blue Book union in Portland when I started on the waterfront. However, there were many men who were union members after the union was broken in 1921. These men formed the backbone of efforts to organize again, even though no formal Blue Book union existed in Portland at that time. The older men in my gang were union men who taught me about the history and struggles of waterfront labor.

The older men on the waterfront often talked about the 1922 strike, though they sometimes referred to the final union break as happening in 1921. They explained how the strikes failed because each port had separate contracts, so when one port struck, others kept working, which broke the strike's effectiveness. From their stories, I learned the importance of organizing coastwise so all ports would strike together and prevent employers from playing unions against each other.

The Industrial Workers of the World, or IWW, were often discussed among the older men on the waterfront. They were known for being very good at organizing workers industrially rather than by craft, but their refusal to engage in political action was seen as a major pitfall. This lack of political involvement meant that gains they made economically were often lost through unfavorable legislation, which taught me the importance of political action in labor struggles.

The Palmer Raids in the early 1920s had a big impact on labor, especially the more militant workers. Many were jailed or deported during these raids, which kept labor in turmoil and weakened union efforts. This repression was part of the difficult environment we faced when trying to organize workers on the waterfront and elsewhere.

The Cuddy Packing Company was a meat packing plant in Portland where the owners sold the business to members of the Cuddy administration and reopened it paying workers half the previous wages. This was a clear example of union busting and exploitation of workers, similar to what was happening in other industries and regions. It showed how employers tried to undermine unions by cutting wages and using scab labor.

After the Vietnam War, many Vietnamese immigrants came to the United States, some of whom were used as strikebreakers or scab labor in industries like mining and packing. This was ironic because these immigrants had helped the U.S. during the war but were then used to undermine union efforts here. It was frustrating to see the government allow this while claiming to support unions abroad.

Poland was mentioned as a place where the U.S. government claimed to support union organizing, yet here at home they were breaking unions. Polish workers were being brought into the U.S. as immigrants and sometimes used as strikebreakers, which was contradictory to the supposed support for labor rights in Poland. It was hard to understand why they couldn't protect their own unions at home as we did here.

The Wall Street Journal published editorials praising Polish workers for their right to strike, yet the very next day it denounced the Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization for striking in the U.S. This hypocrisy was typical of the media's stance on unions, supporting them abroad but opposing them at home. It was pitiful to see such double standards while union membership in the U.S. was at an all-time low.

The Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization, or PATCO, went on strike and was denounced by the media and government, unlike Polish public workers who were praised for striking. This double standard highlighted the low level of union support in the U.S. and the challenges unions faced. PATCO's strike was a significant example of the difficulties labor unions encountered in asserting their rights.

Before the Wagner Act was passed, it was very hard to hold union membership because employers controlled hiring and actively tried to eliminate union men. After the Wagner Act came into effect, it provided legal protections that helped us maintain union membership and organize more effectively. The Act was a turning point in labor history, giving workers more rights and protections on the waterfront and beyond.

Though I started on the Portland waterfront, I transferred to Seattle in the early 1950s. In Portland, we had a man named Jack O'Neil who was actually working for the employers to control hiring and break the union. Seattle had similar struggles with employer control and union organizing, and the tactics used to break unions were consistent across the region. My history is mostly with the Columbia River area, but Seattle was part of the broader waterfront labor story.

The Admiral Line was the preference job on the waterfront, meaning those workers got priority for work and minimum pay guarantees. During the 1934 strike, some of the employer-hired goon squads were given preference jobs on the Admiral Line to help break the union. The minimum pay was two hours, which was a small but important guarantee for workers during tough times.

Senate Bill 1182 was a legislative effort to take away the gains we made in laws protecting workers from accidents. I was personally hurt before the 1934 strike, straining my back, and the rules then were harsh—you had to be off six weeks to get any pay. Bills like 1182 threaten to roll back protections we've fought hard for, which is why political action is so important for workers.
